chapters.title: Step one: pick exactly who you want to serve
chapters.text:
You need to know your target market very well. Many businesses miss this point. Ask the average business owner who their market is, and they will say something like, I sell men clothes, so my target market is any adult man who can afford them.

That is too broad, and it is the reason many businesses fail, especially online.

If a business grows with a market that broad, two things happen. The owner never makes the most money possible, because they are not serving the right people. They also get worn out trying to please everyone. Running a business is hard, so do not make it harder by refusing to narrow your market.

Not everyone is your customer. Pin this to your office wall so you never forget it.

In an online business, detailed targeting is not optional. Without it, you will struggle to write marketing messages that attract and nurture an audience. We will dig into ads and marketing soon, but first, let us get your audience right. Here is what to do.

Build a clear picture of your ideal customer. Write down the traits of the customer you want to work with. Who are they? What do they like? What are their pain points and passions? How much do they earn each year? Where do they live, or does location not matter? Use a notebook to capture all of it. The exercise can feel silly, but it can change your business in a big way.

Pull your ideal customers in with strong offers. Once you know who they are and where to find them, the rest gets easier. For example, if your business helps middle-aged wrestlers with skin care, you know a free skin care guide will be a good hook, because they are curious to try something new.

Now you know how to find your ideal customers. The next chapters will show you how to build a value ladder and move customers up it, how to build a sales funnel that converts, and what it takes to run strong marketing campaigns.

chapters.title: Move customers up your value ladder step by step
chapters.text:
Every business should have a dream result it wants to deliver to its ideal customers. Business is not mainly about products or services. It is about solving problems. A dentist solves dental problems, not just fixes teeth. A makeup artist is in the beauty business, not just putting on makeup. A barber is in the grooming business, not just cutting hair.

Do you see the pattern? Many people let their product or service box them in, but to make the most money, you have to think bigger. Picture the highest result you could deliver, then build a value ladder around it. Not everyone can afford the top tier, and even those who can will not buy right away. You have to walk them up the ladder. A value ladder usually runs from bait, to value one, to value two or more, to the peak value, and then to a continuity program.

The bait pulls them in. It is usually free or nearly free but still useful.

You cannot just ask strangers to buy from you. You have to draw them in with a hook and a strong first offer.

Here is an offline example. If you run a beauty salon, you could post an ad that says, anyone who comes in tomorrow gets a free hair treatment. That is the bait. When they show up, you can offer to sell a hair growth product to the people who need it, or you can offer to style their hair. That is a higher level at a price. Then you can offer makeup or manicure services. Your peak value could be a full beauty and hair care package. Not everyone will buy it, but if your ad brought in the right leads, some will. For a continuity program, you can offer an annual fee for all their treatments, or monthly payments. The goal is to turn them into repeat customers and build a steady income.

The value ladder works for any business. You just need to think it through and come up with offers your ideal market cannot turn down.

chapters.title: Meet your market where they already are
chapters.text:
Most business owners do not have time to handle marketing on their own. There is too much to do, so they hand it to an in-house team or a marketing agency they trust. If you are in this camp, you have probably been told that online marketing, when done well, is more powerful than traditional marketing, but you do not know exactly why.

The secret is targeting.

Online marketing lets you get very specific about who you reach. For example, you can target mothers of children with autism who love medicine, read Psychology Today often, and have spent five to ten thousand dollars online in the last thirty days. You can be as specific as you want. People like to see messages that feel like they were written for them, and that is not possible with radio, TV, or print.

Build ads that speak directly to the people you want to reach.

Two secrets make ad campaigns work. First, research your market so well that you know it inside and out. Understand how your future customers act online and offline. Know where they gather, what their pain points are, and how your business can help. This understanding will help you shape your ad and, just as importantly, decide where to run it.

Second, make your visuals and headlines catchy enough to stop people in their tracks and earn the click. It is hard at first, but you learn by doing.

As Dan Kennedy has said, the business that can spend the most to win a customer wins.

Russell Brunson is the author of this approach.

chapters.title: Successful online businesses master the traffic game
chapters.text:
There are three types of online traffic, and most non-marketers do not know the difference. Paying close attention to each type is the secret to high conversions. The three types are traffic you own, traffic you control, and traffic you do not control.

Traffic you own is the best kind. It is your email list, your followers, your readers, and your customers. You own it because you can reach them directly without paying Google, Facebook, or any other ad platform.

Having followers or an email list that you can reach at will saves you the cost of running ads.

Traffic you control is any kind of paid traffic. If you run an ad on Facebook, you do not own that traffic, Facebook does. But you get to choose what you want the people in that traffic to do, so you can say you control it. For example, you can send them to a landing page, a blog post, or an online store. The catch is that you have to pay every time you need it, so it is smart to convert these leads into traffic you own. Ask for emails or phone numbers in exchange for something useful.

Traffic you do not control just shows up. You do not own it and you do not control it. When someone searches on Google and your site appears, they might land on a blog post and either keep exploring or leave. You cannot control them. The same is true for social media, unless you are running an ad. Your goal with traffic you do not control and traffic you control is to turn them into traffic you own.

chapters.title: Get emails opened with the building blocks of persuasion
chapters.text:
The average person is on ten to twenty email lists and gets email every day. Keep that in mind when you write to your subscribers.

Many email formats work, but behind every strong email sequence are timeless principles of persuasion. Big companies hire expert copywriters to write their emails and earn high click-through rates. You may not have the budget for a copywriter, and that is fine. With practice, you can learn to write emails that get opened.

Your subject line matters a lot. It is the first thing readers see, and they decide whether to open the email based on it. A subject line that sparks curiosity, stuns them, or promises something useful earns plenty of clicks.

After the subject line comes the body. The best emails build a connection, because people like you, look forward to hearing from you, and buy from you when they can relate. Email is a great place to build that connection. Do not worry about being perfect. Be real and make your emails relatable. Are you writing to new freelancers? What lessons from your own journey can you share? How can you weave useful lessons into a story while pointing them to your offer without sounding like a sales pitch?

To avoid sounding salesy, help your audience see the problem they have and offer a solution, which can be your product or service.

It takes time to learn to persuade people to act, but the more you do it, the better you get. Some people wonder how often to send emails. Send as often as you can, ideally no more than once a day and at least once a week. This keeps you fresh in your reader mind without becoming annoying.

Russell Brunson has said that a confused mind always says no.

chapters.title: Build sales funnels that match the needs of your audience
chapters.text:
Do not jump straight into landing pages, ad copy, and hot offers. All of that matters, but it is not the first step. The first step is to figure out what kind of traffic you will send to your landing page. There are three kinds of traffic here.

Hot traffic is made up of people who already know you. They are your customers, followers, or email subscribers. They know your business and they like the value you offer. It is easy to get them to act. You do not have to do much. Just tell them the value of what you are offering, and they will jump on it.

Warm traffic comes from people who have heard about you from someone else. For example, if a social media influencer talks about your product in a video and asks their followers to check you out, that is warm traffic. The same is true when someone recommends your product in a Facebook group or in a blog post because they used it and liked it.

The key to earning recommendations is to do good work.

Cold traffic is made up of people who do not know you at all, so you have to go out of your way to win them over with testimonials or free trials. If you are smart, you will send cold traffic to a different landing page than the other kinds.

A 2019 report from Portent found that website conversion rates drop by an average of four point four two percent for every extra second of load time.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Your online reputation matters for sales. Before the average person buys from a business, they do a quick search to see what others have said. Imagine they find your business, and the top results are negative reviews from bloggers. That is bad for business. That is why you need to put work into making sure your online reputation is strong. You cannot control what others say, but you can control your service and the quality of your products. When you wow people, they feel grateful and leave good reviews on social media, on their blogs, or as testimonials. Fast customer service is another way to earn good reviews. There are many ways to offer support, and using social media as a customer service tool works well because it has two benefits. You can answer customers quickly, and you can build an audience for your company on the same channel. Social media can be part of your service tool kit, or the only tool you use, depending on where your business is and how much you can invest.

If you run a company blog, turn each page into a mini landing page. Use pop-ups to share your bait on every page. Your ideal customers will find the bait interesting, click, and convert from traffic you do not control into traffic you own.
